title: The Gaelic Athlete, vol. VII, no. 182, Saturday June 26th 1915

pages.text:
June 26, 1915

Ireland's Record in Athletic Sports

From the Tailtean Games to the Present Time

Including the Complete History of the Rise and Development of the G.A.A.

By "VIGILANT."

XIX.-The War of 1855 (continued).

WHILE the progress of the Gaelic athletic association in Ireland was attracting the attention of the British press, the public opinion of the country was going to be influenced by the Irish Nationalist's idea of a new system of sport. The Gaels, however, had no inclination to criticise the state of things that permitted athletic sports and football matches to be made a business calling and a means of their gambling on a wholesale scale throughout England. On the contrary, the correct thing for Ireland to do, according to her self-constituted public instructors, was to go on imitating England's idea of sport as had been the case up to 1884. At the very time when this pharosical fault was so energetically directed against Ireland's National Athletic Association that was to be the first in the world, the Gaels were almost entirely dependent upon the public opinion of the country. In 1888 the football players raised a terrible clamour for payment and the cry increased in 1884, and gained strength by the action of the Press. The meeting of the Football Association (Soccer) at the Freemason's Tavern, London, on January 19th, 1885, brought this "fight for honest payment," as it was called, to very close quarters. The men who believed in preserving sport for the sake of a public opinion that would bring about a lapse from all participation in outdoor sports, except as passive patrons, opposed the resolution for legalizing of professionalism. Mr. Charles Crump, who was then a great force in English football circles, opposed the resolution by an amendment which he proposed to an amendment which he proposed to an amendment which he proposed
